holding: Created the automobile exception: officers with probable cause may search a movable vehicle without a warrant because its mobility makes obtaining one impracticable. A Prohibition-era recalibration of the warrant requirement to new technology (the automobile) and new enforcement demands.
point_cited_for: Automobile exception; Prohibition-era recalibration of the Fourth Amendment

holding: Wiretapping that involved no physical trespass into the defendant's premises and seized no tangible thing was not a Fourth Amendment search or seizure. The trespass/property-based view of the Amendment - later overruled by Katz. (Brandeis's dissent foreshadowed the privacy approach.)
point_cited_for: Wiretap = no trespass = no search (the rule Katz later recalibrated)

holding: The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places; a search occurs when the government violates a person's reasonable expectation of privacy. Overruled Olmstead's trespass requirement (electronic eavesdropping on a phone-booth call was a search). Harlan's concurrence supplies the two-part REP test.
point_cited_for: REP test; recalibration of Olmstead from trespass to privacy

holding: A Coast Guard officer's use of a searchlight to illuminate the deck of a boat on the high seas, revealing cases of liquor, was not a search; use of a searchlight is not prohibited and reveals only what is in open view. Early sense-enhancement-is-not-a-search case (precursor to the Kyllo line).
point_cited_for: Coast Guard searchlight not a search; early sense-enhancement principle

holding: Police generally may not, without a warrant, search digital information on a cell phone seized incident to arrest; the search-incident-to-arrest exception (Robinson) does not extend to the vast trove of data on a modern smartphone. Recalibrated the SITA doctrine for digital devices.
point_cited_for: Cell-phone data; limit on search incident to arrest (Robinson -> Riley)
correction: Reporter quirk: analyze_citations did not resolve '573 U.S. 373' by US-reporter string, but the merits opinion is verified: cluster/opinion id 2680439, decided 2014-06-25 (Roberts), parallel 134 S. Ct. 2473 / 573 U.S. 373. CL slug is 'riley-v-cal-united-states'. The cert-stage orders (134 S. Ct. 999; 134 S. Ct. 1870) are DIFFERENT clusters - do not cite those for the merits.

holding: The core of the Fourth Amendment (security against arbitrary intrusion) is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause - BUT the federal exclusionary rule was NOT yet imposed on the states (states free to choose their own remedy). Later overruled on the remedy point by Mapp.
point_cited_for: Fourth Amendment incorporated against states (1949); pre-Mapp remedy gap
correction: Nuance for the 'until 1949 it applied only to the federal government' point: Wolf incorporated the Amendment's substance against the states in 1949, but did NOT extend the exclusionary remedy - that came with Mapp (1961). State the two-step accurately.

holding: The Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment; evidence obtained by an unconstitutional search/seizure is inadmissible in state court. Overruled Wolf on the remedy and completed incorporation.
point_cited_for: Exclusionary rule incorporated against the states (completes the incorporation arc)

cite: Milam v. United States, 296 F. 629 (4th Cir. 1924)
court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Fourth Circuit
holding: Early Prohibition-era Fourth Circuit decision on warrantless search of an automobile for contraband liquor - part of the pre-Carroll circuit case law that the Supreme Court surveyed and relied on in Carroll v. United States (1925) when it recognized the automobile exception.
point_cited_for: Early automobile/Prohibition search case in the Carroll lineage (recalibration)
correction: IDENTIFICATION: manifest listed 'Milam v. United States - UNIDENTIFIED.' Identified as Milam v. United States, 296 F. 629 (4th Cir. Feb. 8, 1924) - a Prohibition-era automobile-search case cited within Carroll. Existence/cite/court/year VERIFIED in CourtListener; full opinion text was a stub (cluster/opinion id gotcha), so the precise holding above is sourced from its role in Carroll and secondary description - writer should spot-confirm the specific facts before quoting.

holding: Cross-link to T4: CSLI acquisition is a search; the third-party doctrine does not extend to comprehensive digital location records. The modern endpoint of the recalibration arc - the Court adapting the Fourth Amendment to digital technology.
point_cited_for: Modern recalibration to digital tech (cross-link to T4; verified once, reused)

holding: Cross-link to T4: thermal imaging of a home with technology not in general public use is a search. Shows the recalibration principle applied to sense-enhancing technology, continuing the line from United States v. Lee (searchlight) to modern devices.
point_cited_for: Sense-enhancing tech recalibration (cross-link to T4; verified once, reused)
